Harvey Software Engineer, Agents
Office Location
New York

Job Type
Full Time

Team
Engineering

Compensation Range
$161,300 – $241,900 USD (Offers Equity)

Apply Now
Why Harvey
At Harvey, we’re transforming how legal and professional services operate. By combining frontier agentic AI, an enterprise-grade platform, and deep domain expertise, we’re reshaping how critical knowledge work gets done for decades to come.

This is a rare chance to help build a generational company at a true inflection point. With 1500+ customers in 60+ countries, strong product-market fit, and world-class investor support, we’re scaling fast and defining a new category in real time. The work is ambitious, the bar is high, and the opportunity for growth — personal, professional, and financial — is unmatched.

Our team moves fast, takes ownership, and is deeply committed to the mission — operating with intensity, staying close to our customers, and pushing each other for excellence. We live by three values: Decisiveness, Simplicity, and Job's Not Finished. We act quickly on clear judgment over perfect information, we believe simplicity is what scales, and we're never satisfied with where we are. If you want to do the best work of your career alongside people who share that drive, we'd love to build with you.

At Harvey, the future of professional services is being written today — and we’re just getting started.

Role Overview
As a Software Engineer, Agents, you'll build the systems that make our AI agents indispensable to legal professionals.

You will design environments and actions for agentic professional work, make model selection decisions, manage context windows, create optimal tools, and develop evals that enable faster iteration loops to unlock new capabilities.

We're looking for engineers and researchers who are immersed in the space, driven to ship impactful products, and are experienced in using practical evaluations to drive task completion quality and customer delight.

Representative Projects
Save weeks of effort on high pressure M&A deals by automating information requests and diligence checks across hundreds of thousands of files with retrieval and file editing agents.

Dramatically speed up deal turnaround speed by improving the latency and quality of agents on applying a standard legal "playbook" to contracts.

Ensure that our clients win their complex, data-hungry litigation cases by optimizing our multi-source retrieval agents.

Enable board-ready PowerPoint generation by tuning the harness and libraries for coding agents.

What You'll Do
Partner with customers and PMs to understand legal workflows, design practical evaluations that capture what “excellent” means, and ship agents that get the job done.

Optimize agent performance through prompt engineering, model selection, tool design, skill writing, context window management, and eval harness development.

Work with our model infra team to design and implement infrastructure for low-latency agent execution, including caching strategies, parallel tool calls, or subagent patterns.

Improve our observability and instrumentation to profile agent behavior, identify bottlenecks, and drive optimization decisions.

Stay current on new developments in agentic systems and bring those learnings back to the products we build.

What You Have
Passion for building effective domain-specific agents.

Iterative mindset: you develop proof of concepts, make decisions quickly, and ship v0s.

Comfortable with when and how to use evaluations to drive quality.

Humble and adaptable about code and frameworks. We expect you to drive adoption of new best practices as they develop.

2-5 years (post-BS/MS) of software engineering experience.

We are hiring for this role across Mid, Senior, and Staff levels.

Proficiency in Python and experience working with LLM APIs and agent frameworks.

Experience with shipping user-facing products, either on the backend or full-stack.

An Overview of Harvey AI's Features for Lawyers: # An Overview of Harvey AI’s Features for Lawyers. Harvey AI stands out as a platform engineered specifically for legal professionals, offering a large language model (LLM) that can be trained on a legal team’s proprietary document library. By fine-tuning its LLM’s training on a legal team’s proprietary documents, it can interpret legal queries with nuance, recognize legal terminology, and provide tailored responses that increase the likelihood of meeting the expectations and rigor of attorneys. In fact, Harvey AI has a robust and comprehensive Security Addendum as part of its partnership with legal teams that can be reviewed on its website. Harvey’s History tool is designed so that the user can access all previous prompts, queries, and documents uploaded into the tool, as well as responses by the tool. The integration of an AI legal tool like Harvey AI into a legal practice is a natural evolution of how lawyers are interacting with technology for the betterment of their practice and the benefit of their client.

The platform includes both pre-built workflows for common legal processes and the ability for users to build custom workflows tailored to their practice. Enables users to query documents and request analysis without uploading files. The platform includes a library of pre-built query templates as well as the option to create private or shared organizational prompt libraries. Allows users to run pre-built AI agent workflows or create custom workflows without requiring coding. External MCP-compatible applications can connect to Harvey to access legal Q&A, analyze documents stored in Vault, and query specialized research knowledge sources. Harvey also announced a partnership with OpenAI to create a custom-trained case law model meant to assist with legal tasks such as drafting documents, answering questions about complex litigation scenarios, and identifying material discrepances between hundreds of contracts.

Great. Now I have a solid picture of Harvey. Let me give you a concrete breakdown of what a day-to-day would look like for a Software Engineer working on the Agents team.

## **Company Overview**

**Harvey** is a generative AI platform purpose-built for law firms and corporate legal teams. Founded around 2022, it's raised significant funding—most recently $300M in Series D (Feb 2025) at a $3B valuation. With 1,500+ customers across 60+ countries, they're scaling rapidly. The company partners with top-tier law firms (like A&O Shearman) and in-house legal teams to automate complex legal workflows using AI agents. They've just launched 500+ pre-built agents and an Agent Builder tool, and they're actively collaborating with OpenAI on custom models for legal reasoning.

**Engineering culture**: Described as "Decisiveness, Simplicity, Job's Not Finished." They move fast, ship v0s, and stay close to customers. High bar, high intensity.

---

## **A Realistic Day-to-Day for a Software Engineer, Agents**

### **Morning (~9 AM – 12 PM)**

Your day likely starts with **async context-setting**: checking Slack for overnight updates, reviewing any new customer feedback or bug reports from the previous evening. You're part of an agents-focused squad, so you might have a **10:30 AM standup** (15-20 min) with 4-6 engineers, a product manager, and possibly a legal domain expert. You discuss: What shipped? What broke? What's blocking progress?

**Concrete tasks in this window:**

- **Review a PR**: A colleague built a tool that calls a legal document extraction API. You read their prompt engineering approach, check how they're handling timeouts and caching. You discuss context-window management—do we truncate docs or chunk them? This feeds directly into agent quality.
- **Evaluate yesterday's agent runs**: You pull metrics from an internal dashboard. A customer complained that an M&A diligence agent missed some clauses in 500-file document sets. You examine the evaluation logs: retrieval was 85% accurate, but the agent didn't retry on ambiguous queries. You identify a quick fix: adjust the tool-calling logic to trigger a secondary pass.

### **Late Morning (12 PM – 1 PM)**

**Customer-adjacent work**: A customer success manager forwards a Slack message: a top-tier firm is building a new contract review agent, and they want to understand context-window tradeoffs. You jump on a **brief sync** (20 min, often async-first culture but this is a blocker). You explain: GPT-4 has 128k tokens, but for legal docs, we often use a retrieval layer to keep context tight and latency low. You sketch a design on a shared doc and commit to a POC by EOW.

Alternatively: **Prompt iteration session**. You're tuning the reasoning prompt for a litigation agent that needs to cross-reference case law. You test a few variations in a Jupyter notebook, measure success against your eval suite (e.g., "did it cite the right precedent 90%+ of the time?"). This is hands-on experimentation—not glamorous, but crucial for product quality.

### **Afternoon (~1 PM – 5 PM)**

**Core coding work**:

- **Implementing a new tool or skill**: Your PM prioritized adding a "regulatory compliance cross-check" capability to the M&A agent. You write a Python function that wraps a compliance knowledge base API, handles pagination, and returns structured results. You're using LangChain or similar frameworks (Harvey likely has internal abstractions on top of this). You write tests and think about failure modes: What if the API is slow? What if a clause is outside the compliance scope?
  
- **Optimization**: You're profiling agent latency. The board-ready PowerPoint agent is taking 8 seconds per slide. You trace the issue: the model is making sequential tool calls when it could parallelize. You refactor the agent harness to support parallel tool invocation, test it, and cut latency to 3 seconds. You measure token usage before/after—cost matters at scale.

- **Eval framework development**: You're building a new evaluation suite for a litigation agent. You write code to:
  - Spin up a corpus of 50 anonymized case scenarios.
  - Run your agent against each.
  - Compare outputs against lawyer-verified ground truth (provided by Harvey's legal domain experts).
  - Calculate metrics: precision, recall, F1 score on key fact extraction.
  - You identify that the agent performs poorly on "complex multi-party disputes" and tag that for a follow-up sprint.

**Collaboration**: You might pair with:
- **Model Infra engineer**: Discussing caching strategies. Should we cache embeddings from retrieved docs to speed up subsequent queries on the same file?
- **Legal domain expert** (often an attorney on staff): They review your eval framework to ensure it captures what "good" looks like legally.
- **PM**: Syncing on prioritization. Does optimizing latency or improving accuracy matter more right now? (Likely both, but trade-off calls happen weekly.)

### **Late Afternoon (~5 PM – 6 PM)**

- **Async documentation / knowledge sharing**: You write a brief design doc on your parallel tool-calling optimization, post it in a shared Wiki or Notion, and ping the team. This is part of Harvey's "ship and communicate" ethos.
- **Checking in on a deployment**: An agent you shipped last week went live for a customer. You monitor a dashboard (likely custom-built or built on top of LangSmith / similar): Are users calling the agent? Are quality metrics stable? Is there a latency spike? You're on-call for quick fixes.

---

## **Typical Weekly Rhythms**

- **2–3 stand-ups** (daily or 3x/week, depending on team cadence)
- **1 design review**: You or a peer present a new agent architecture or evaluation strategy to the broader team.
- **1–2 customer conversations**: Either directly or with a PM/Legal Engineer. Real-world feedback ("the agent hallucinates when contracts are in French") drives priorities.
- **Code review time**: ~20% of your day, ensuring quality across the agent codebase.
- **Experimentation**: ~40% building and tuning agents, evals, tools.
- **Infrastructure/optimization**: ~30% working with infra teams on caching, model selection, latency.
- **On-call rotation**: Likely 1 week per sprint, monitoring production agents for failures.

---

## **Key Tools & Tech Stack (Inferred)**

- **Languages**: Python (primary)
- **LLM APIs**: OpenAI (GPT-4, and likely their custom case-law model), possibly Claude or other APIs for comparison
- **Agent frameworks**: Likely LangChain, or Harvey has built internal abstractions on top of it
- **Observability**: Custom dashboards, possibly LangSmith for monitoring, internal logging for eval data
- **Evaluation**: Custom Python eval harnesses, likely some use of frameworks like Ragas or custom metrics
- **Infrastructure**: Likely cloud-based (AWS/GCP), with careful attention to cost and latency
- **Version control**: Git, GitHub
- **Communication**: Slack, shared docs (Notion/Confluence)

---

## **What Makes This Role Different from Typical Backend Engineering**

1. **You're shipping ML products, not traditional software**: Evals, prompt tuning, and model selection are *your* tools. PRs include prompt changes, not just code changes.
2. **Customer feedback loops are tight**: A lawyer notices the agent misses a clause type → you build an eval → you iterate the agent → it ships in days, not quarters.
3. **You work across domains**: You need enough legal knowledge to reason about workflows (M&A, litigation, compliance), but you're not expected to be a lawyer.
4. **High ambiguity early**: You're defining what "good" means for agents that didn't exist a year ago. This is both creative and challenging.
5. **Urgency**: Legal professionals bill by the hour. If you save them a week on a deal, the ROI is immediate and measurable. This creates momentum and accountability.

---

**Bottom line**: You'd spend your time building, evaluating, and optimizing AI agents that solve real legal problems. It's fast-paced, technically deep, and grounded in customer impact. The compensation ($161–242K + equity) reflects Harvey's stage and the fact that you're working on a genuinely high-leverage product in a hot market.

WRITER is committed to transparent, market-based compensation practices. Compensation offered will be determined by multiple factors such as role scope and complexity, location, experience, knowledge, and skills. Cash compensation is only one part of WRITER’s competitive Total Rewards package, which can also include equity, an immersive purpose-driven culture, career development, and thoughtfully designed benefits and well-being offerings.

Overview
Application
🚀 About WRITER

WRITER is where the world's leading enterprises orchestrate AI-powered work. Our vision is to expand human capacity through superintelligence. And we're proving it's possible – through powerful, trustworthy AI that unites IT and business teams together to unlock enterprise-wide transformation. With WRITER's end-to-end platform, hundreds of companies like Mars, Marriott, Uber, and Vanguard are building and deploying AI agents that are grounded in their company's data and fueled by WRITER's enterprise-grade LLMs. Valued at $1.9B and backed by industry-leading investors including Premji Invest, Radical Ventures, and ICONIQ Growth, WRITER is rapidly cementing its position as the leader in enterprise generative AI.

Founded in 2020 with office hubs in San Francisco, New York City, Seattle, Austin, Chicago, and London, our team thinks big and moves fast, and we're looking for smart, hardworking builders and scalers to join us on our journey to create a better future of work with AI.

📐 About the rolex

When architecting memory for an AI agent, keep in mind that vector databases excel at handling unstructured, high-dimensional data and are well suited to similarity search, whereas graph RAG is advantageous for representing entities and explicit relationships when those relationships are crucial.

## A Realistic Day-to-Day for a Software Engineer (Generative AI) at WRITER

### What You'd Actually Build

WRITER is a 200-500 person, well-funded ($326M raised, $1.9B valuation, Nov 2024) enterprise platform where large companies (Uber, Vanguard, Mars, Marriott) deploy **AI agents**—autonomous systems that plan and execute multi-step workflows across the company's systems and data. Your job is to engineer the backend infrastructure, APIs, and services that power those agents at scale.

You're not building chatbots. You're building the **supervised AI orchestration layer** that lets a Vanguard agent autonomously research investment opportunities across internal databases, cross-reference multiple data sources via their graph-based RAG system, make recommendations, and log everything with audit trails for compliance.

---

### A Typical Week

**Monday Morning: Standup + Planning**
- Your team (8–12 engineers reporting to an eng director or senior manager) meets async or in-person (hybrid in SF/NYC/Seattle) to discuss the sprint
- You review PRs from Friday; two of them involve changes to the LLM Gateway (WRITER's dynamic model-routing layer) and a new vector database integration point
- Your current project: optimizing latency on the agent execution service, which orchestrates LLM calls, tool invocations, and multi-step reasoning for enterprise workflows
- You meet with your product manager to clarify requirements for a new enterprise feature: real-time agent supervision dashboards that let admins pause/modify agent decisions mid-execution

**Tuesday–Thursday: Deep Engineering**

Your day involves:
- **Building agentic services in Python + FastAPI**: You're implementing a new microservice that sits between the agent orchestration layer and the knowledge graph (WRITER's graph-based RAG, which stores enterprise data as structured entities and relationships instead of vectors). The service needs to fetch context, validate it against RBAC rules, and return results in <50ms even under load.
  
- **Architecture decisions with a "whole-systems mindset"**: You design how this service integrates with existing microservices, how it handles failures, where to add circuit breakers, caching layers. You write a 2-page architecture doc and socialize it async in Slack before starting code.
  
- **Async/concurrency patterns**: Using FastAPI and Python's asyncio, you optimize concurrent LLM calls. An agent might need to invoke 5 tools in parallel (fetch from Salesforce, check permissions, query vector store, validate data quality, log event)—all non-blocking. You benchmark with locust to verify <100ms p99 latency under 1000 concurrent agents.
  
- **Collaborating with DevOps/Platform**: You pair with a platform engineer to design how this service deploys to Kubernetes clusters across multiple regions. You define the resource requests, scaling policies, health checks, and CircleCI/GitHub Actions pipeline.
  
- **Security & Enterprise compliance**: The service needs to enforce SSO (SAML/OIDC), RBAC (role-based access control), and audit logging. You implement request signing, validate JWT tokens, log every agent decision with timestamps and user attribution—all so Vanguard's compliance team can audit who did what and when.
  
- **Tooling that actually speeds you up**: You use Claude in Cursor to generate boilerplate for async database queries, skeleton tests, and error handling. You run GitHub Copilot for suggesting patterns for the LLM integration logic (it's surprisingly good at RAG + agent-memory patterns).

**Friday: Integration + Reviews**

- Your service is merged, deployed to staging. You work with a data scientist to test it against realistic agent workflows from a beta customer (e.g., a CPG company automating supply-chain decisions).
- You review two junior engineers' PRs—they're building guardrails (custom validation rules that prevent agents from hallucinating or taking unsafe actions). You suggest using WRITER's open-source guardrails library to avoid duplication.
- Retrospective: the week's work reduced agent execution latency by 23% on average, but you surfaced a new issue with vector database timeouts under concurrent load. You file a ticket for next sprint.

---

### Typical Meetings (Per Week)

- **Standup** (3x weekly, 15 min): async Slack thread or brief sync call
- **Product sync** (1x): clarifying requirements, discussing roadmap impact
- **Architecture review** (1x): whiteboarding design with a staff engineer before major changes
- **Cross-functional pairing** (2x): with DevOps (deployment), data scientists (agent fine-tuning), or front-end engineers (they need APIs for the supervision dashboard)
- **On-call rotation**: you're on call 1 week per month; if an agent fails in production (e.g., malformed LLM output), you debug logs in Datadog, roll back or hotfix, and post-mortem

---

### Your Core Stack in Practice

- **Python**: 70% of your day—FastAPI services, async database access (asyncpg for PostgreSQL), integrating with LLMs via OpenAI/Anthropic SDKs, vector DB libraries (pgvector, Pinecone client)
- **PostgreSQL**: storing agent state, execution history, audit logs; pgvector extension for dense vector queries when needed
- **React/TypeScript**: you might not write frontend code daily, but you design APIs *for* the supervision dashboard—so you're thinking about the shape of data, real-time updates via WebSockets, pagination
- **Kubernetes + Docker**: your service runs in K8s across AWS/GCP; you write the Dockerfile and helm charts
- **LLM integrations**: You call Claude, GPT-4, and WRITER's proprietary Palmyra LLMs via APIs; you handle prompt engineering, token budgets, temperature tuning based on use case
- **Graph-based RAG**: You integrate queries into WRITER's Knowledge Graph—not traditional vector search, but structured entity/relationship lookups with MCP (Model Context Protocol) to keep context fresh
- **Monitoring**: DataDog for logs, metrics (latency, error rates per agent type, token usage), traces; PagerDuty for alerts

---

### Key Collaborators

1. **Product Manager**: defining what agents should do, which customers are priority, which new capabilities unblock revenue
2. **Data Scientists**: fine-tuning LLMs, building guardrails, testing agent accuracy on real workflows
3. **DevOps/Platform**: scaling, secrets management (rotating LLM API keys without downtime), monitoring
4. **Security/Enterprise**: SSO setup, compliance logging, audit trails for enterprise customers
5. **Front-end engineers**: designing APIs for the agent supervision UI, debugging integration issues
6. **Other backend engineers**: code review, architectural discussions, shared patterns for agentic workflows

---

### The Reality Check

**What matches the job description:**
- ✅ You absolutely need solid Python + FastAPI + async patterns (this is core)
- ✅ Generative AI is embedded in everything—every service talks to LLMs or RAG systems
- ✅ You're building microservices, APIs, integrating cloud platforms (AWS/GCP), and deploying to K8s
- ✅ The "whole-systems mindset" is real: you own services end-to-end from proposal to production
- ✅ Security/enterprise compliance (RBAC, SSO, audit) is front-and-center, not an afterthought
- ✅ Cross-functional collaboration is constant

**What you should know:**
- This is a **fast-moving growth-stage company** (200-500 people, raised $326M). You'll face competing priorities, incomplete specs, and occasional leadership reshuffles (per Glassdoor feedback).
- **RAG and agentic AI are still evolving**: WRITER's shift from vector search to graph-based RAG is relatively recent. You'll be learning and iterating, not just implementing a settled architecture.
- The **"Connect, Challenge, Own" culture** is genuinely pushed here (they made Inc.'s Best Workplaces 2024), but you'll still see the typical tensions of a scaling startup: hiring ramp-up, occasional process churn, demands to ship fast.
- **Compensation is strong** ($124k–$304k SF/NYC depending on level and experience), plus equity in a well-funded company.

This is a high-leverage role: the code you write directly enables Fortune 500 companies to automate mission-critical workflows, so impact is real and measurable.

- **Collaborating with DevOps/Platform**: You pair with a platform engineer to design how this service deploys to Kubernetes clusters across multiple regions. You define the resource requests, scaling policies, health checks, and CircleCI/GitHub Actions pipeline.
  
- **Security & Enterprise compliance**: The service needs to enforce SSO (SAML/OIDC), RBAC (role-based access control), and audit logging. You implement request signing, validate JWT tokens, log every agent decision with timestamps and user attribution—all so Vanguard's compliance team can audit who did what and when.
  
- **Tooling that actually speeds you up**: You use Claude in Cursor to generate boilerplate for async database queries, skeleton tests, and error handling. You run GitHub Copilot for suggesting patterns for the LLM integration logic (it's surprisingly good at RAG + agent-memory patterns).

**Friday: Integration + Reviews**

- Your service is merged, deployed to staging. You work with a data scientist to test it against realistic agent workflows from a beta customer (e.g., a CPG company automating supply-chain decisions).
- You review two junior engineers' PRs—they're building guardrails (custom validation rules that prevent agents from hallucinating or taking unsafe actions). You suggest using WRITER's open-source guardrails library to avoid duplication.
- Retrospective: the week's work reduced agent execution latency by 23% on average, but you surfaced a new issue with vector database timeouts under concurrent load. You file a ticket for next sprint.

---

### Typical Meetings (Per Week)

- **Standup** (3x weekly, 15 min): async Slack thread or brief sync call
- **Product sync** (1x): clarifying requirements, discussing roadmap impact
- **Architecture review** (1x): whiteboarding design with a staff engineer before major changes
- **Cross-functional pairing** (2x): with DevOps (deployment), data scientists (agent fine-tuning), or front-end engineers (they need APIs for the supervision dashboard)
- **On-call rotation**: you're on call 1 week per month; if an agent fails in production (e.g., malformed LLM output), you debug logs in Datadog, roll back or hotfix, and post-mortem

---

### Your Core Stack in Practice

- **Python**: 70% of your day—FastAPI services, async database access (asyncpg for PostgreSQL), integrating with LLMs via OpenAI/Anthropic SDKs, vector DB libraries (pgvector, Pinecone client)
- **PostgreSQL**: storing agent state, execution history, audit logs; pgvector extension for dense vector queries when needed
- **React/TypeScript**: you might not write frontend code daily, but you design APIs *for* the supervision dashboard—so you're thinking about the shape of data, real-time updates via WebSockets, pagination
- **Kubernetes + Docker**: your service runs in K8s across AWS/GCP; you write the Dockerfile and helm charts
- **LLM integrations**: You call Claude, GPT-4, and WRITER's proprietary Palmyra LLMs via APIs; you handle prompt engineering, token budgets, temperature tuning based on use case
- **Graph-based RAG**: You integrate queries into WRITER's Knowledge Graph—not traditional vector search, but structured entity/relationship lookups with MCP (Model Context Protocol) to keep context fresh
- **Monitoring**: DataDog for logs, metrics (latency, error rates per agent type, token usage), traces; PagerDuty for alerts

---

### Key Collaborators

1. **Product Manager**: defining what agents should do, which customers are priority, which new capabilities unblock revenue
2. **Data Scientists**: fine-tuning LLMs, building guardrails, testing agent accuracy on real workflows
3. **DevOps/Platform**: scaling, secrets management (rotating LLM API keys without downtime), monitoring
4. **Security/Enterprise**: SSO setup, compliance logging, audit trails for enterprise customers
5. **Front-end engineers**: designing APIs for the agent supervision UI, debugging integration issues
6. **Other backend engineers**: code review, architectural discussions, shared patterns for agentic workflows

---

### The Reality Check

**What matches the job description:**
- ✅ You absolutely need solid Python + FastAPI + async patterns (this is core)
- ✅ Generative AI is embedded in everything—every service talks to LLMs or RAG systems
- ✅ You're building microservices, APIs, integrating cloud platforms (AWS/GCP), and deploying to K8s
- ✅ The "whole-systems mindset" is real: you own services end-to-end from proposal to production
- ✅ Security/enterprise compliance (RBAC, SSO, audit) is front-and-center, not an afterthought
- ✅ Cross-functional collaboration is constant

**What you should know:**
- This is a **fast-moving growth-stage company** (200-500 people, raised $326M). You'll face competing priorities, incomplete specs, and occasional leadership reshuffles (per Glassdoor feedback).
- **RAG and agentic AI are still evolving**: WRITER's shift from vector search to graph-based RAG is relatively recent. You'll be learning and iterating, not just implementing a settled architecture.

Where did Devin go? What does it say about the future of AI dev tools?: Where did Devin go? What does it say about the future of AI dev tools?
r/ChatGPTCoding - 39 upvotes, 67 comments
I’ve been watching the whole Devin conversation fade out over the past year, and honestly, it’s been fascinating. Remember when it first dropped? Everyone was losing their minds saying it was the end of SWE jobs. Now, it's radio silence. It seems more like the idea just evaporated. The more I talk to other builders, the more a pattern shows up. Devin didn’t fail because the ambition was wrong. It failed because it aimed at a version of autonomy the current models and tooling can’t support yet. You can’t expect a single system to magically understand your repo, rewrite your backend, run migrations, and ship a product without a ton of human constraints wrapped around it. Everyone in those comment sections was saying the same thing. The vision was cool, but the timing was off. I tried a bunch of these agents. The promise was full autonomy, but the reality still involves a lot of babysitting. You give it a task, it goes off the rails, you correct it, it sort of gets back on track. Rinse and repeat. It feels less like replacing me and more like having a really fast, sometimes frustrating intern. The whole thing seemed built for a future where LLMs were just way smarter than what we actually have. Well, let's see how the landscape shifted. Instead of trying to create a replacement engineer, tools started leaning into more realistic strengths. I’ve been testing a bunch of AI dev setups myself. Some are fun for quick demos, some for debugging, some for drafting entire modules. Cursor is doubling down on code editing. Claude is building incredible reasoning chains. DeepSeek is pushing raw speed and cost efficiency. It feels less like one tool needs to do everything and more like people are building proper workflows again. Atoms, a tool that’s been emerging, leans into a multi-agent structure instead of pretending a single model can hold everything in its head. It still needs direction. You still have to review decisions. But the team-style setup makes the output a lot more predictable than relying on one giant agent that tries to guess everything. I don’t mean Claude, Atoms, or anyone else has solved the full autonomy thing. We’re not there yet and probably won’t be for a while. But compared to the Devin approach of give it your repo and pray, the newer tools feel like they’re figuring out how to work with humans rather than replace them. The future probably isn’t a single agent doing the whole job. It’s systems that break the problem into parts and communicate what they’re doing, instead of silently rewriting your app. Has your stack changed since the Devin wave, or did you stick with whatever you were using before? What actually moved the needle for you, if anything? What’s been working for you in the long run?

Based on my research, here's a concrete picture of what a day-to-day as an Applied AI Engineer at Cognition would actually look like:

---

## **The Company & Context**

Cognition is a well-funded ($400M Series C, valued near $2B) applied AI lab with an elite founding team. They built Devin, the AI software engineer that launched in March 2024 and sparked major industry discussion. They're now moving beyond the hype cycle—recently acquiring Windsurf (an agentic IDE) and deploying at "the largest and most complex institutions in the world." This role exists precisely because Devin's value isn't in running demos; it's locked behind figuring out *how* enterprises actually integrate autonomous agents into their workflows. As a first Applied AI Engineer, you're both doing customer work and codifying it into repeatable processes.

---

## **A Realistic Day-to-Day Workflow**

**Monday morning: Account kickoff & environment setup**
- You're embedded with a Fortune 500 engineering team that just signed on. First thing: a 2-hour deep-dive with their 8-person backend team, lead engineer, and an internal champion you'll work with daily.
- They describe their problem: "We're writing a ton of boilerplate microservice integrations—same database patterns, auth flows, API contracts. Takes 2 weeks per service even with senior engineers."
- You ask diagnostic questions: What's their codebase structure? (GitHub monorepo, 400K LOC, TypeScript + Python microservices. DeepWiki automatically indexes it.) Who owns QA? What's their deployment pipeline? (GitHub Actions → staging → production.)
- You take notes, walk back to a co-working space, and spend 2 hours configuring Devin for their stack: setting up the MCP server connections so Devin can query their codebase docs, connecting their GitHub, configuring their CI/CD tools so Devin can trigger tests and see real failures, not guesses.

**Tuesday: Hands-on pilot—pair programming**
- You sit with their most respected senior engineer (he's skeptical, which is perfect). You prompt Devin: *"Write a new microservice for payment processing following the patterns in services/billing and services/orders. Include migrations, error handling, and unit tests."*
- Devin takes 45 minutes to generate a complete, production-ready service. You both watch it work. He spots an issue—it's using an old auth library they've deprecated. 
- You show him that instead of throwing the whole thing out, you can give Devin feedback: *"The auth integration should use lib/new-auth instead."* Devin regenerates that section in 5 minutes.
- You pair-program on a second service together. He starts understanding the workflow: instead of starting from scratch, he can describe intent, Devin drafts, he reviews and refines. Suddenly he's asking, *"Could Devin also generate the API contract doc?"* (It can, via DeepWiki integrations.)
- By EOD, you've shipped a small feature—two new internal APIs—that would've taken him 3 days solo. You've documented this in Slack with before/after metrics (time, lines of code generated, test coverage).

**Wednesday: Workflow architecture**
- The team's QA lead pulls you in. They're thinking bigger: *"We have hundreds of edge-case test scenarios that take forever to write manually. Can Devin generate those?"*
- You spend the day architecting a workflow: Devin can parse their Postman collections and test specs, auto-generate parametric test suites. You configure the MCP integrations so Devin can read their test framework (Jest), their API schemas, and their known bug database.
- You write a small implementation guide (it's now a repeatable playbook for the next customer): "How to Set Up Devin for Test Generation." Screenshots, config snippets, common gotchas.

**Thursday: Enablement session + metrics**
- You run a live 60-minute workshop with 15 engineers from the team (not just your core contact). Agenda:
  - 10 min: Live demo—Devin solving a real problem from their backlog.
  - 20 min: Hands-on—you and a volunteer pair-program on an actual task from their sprint.
  - 20 min: Q&A on adoption workflows, best practices (when to use Devin, when to code manually, how to integrate into their daily standup).
  - 10 min: How to file issues and feedback.
- Afterward, you quantify week one: 2 services shipped (normally 4–5 days of engineering effort), 3 engineers now have Devin installed, 40 test cases auto-generated (normally a QA person's workweek).
- You send a one-pager to the account's business owner: "Applied AI ROI Week 1: ~40 engineering-hours recovered, $X in developer time saved, on track for $Y annualized."

**Friday: Scaling & documentation**
- You spend 3 hours with your manager and the Sales/Applied AI team reviewing the week's learnings. 
  - What worked? (MCP integrations for their specific codebase, pair-programming workshops, having one technical champion who evangelizes internally.)
  - What didn't? (Devin couldn't auto-generate their proprietary ORM queries on first try—needed config.)
  - This becomes a case study: "Microservice Gen Pattern—Step-by-Step Playbook."
- You update a shared Google Drive: customer playbooks, setup scripts, workshop slides. This becomes the knowledge base the next Applied AI Engineer uses for their first week at a similar company.
- You respond to Slack threads from 2 other customers asking questions about Devin setup. One customer wants to know if Devin can integrate with their Jira workflow; you prototype a quick MCP config as a proof-of-concept and loop them in.

---

## **What Your Tools & Collaboration Actually Look Like**

**Daily tools:**
- **Devin** (the core product—you're using it 4–6 hours/day, but as an advisor/orchestrator, not as a user)
- **GitHub** (reading their codebases, understanding their version control)
- **DeepWiki** (auto-generated docs for their repos—you're helping configure what gets indexed)
- **MCP configs** (JSON configs connecting Devin to their APIs, test runners, databases)
- **Their stack** (whatever they use: TypeScript/Python, Jest/pytest, GitHub Actions, Postgres, etc.)
- **Slack** (your primary channel to both the customer team and your internal colleagues)
- **Figma/Excalidraw** (quick diagrams for workflows: "Here's how Devin integrates into your CI/CD")
- **Google Docs/Notion** (playbooks, case studies, metrics tracking)

**Who you collaborate with daily:**
- **Your customer's engineering lead** (the person accountable for adoption; you're making their job easier)
- **QA, DevOps, or platform engineers** (whoever has specific pain points Devin can solve)
- **Sales/AE at Cognition** (they own the relationship; you're the implementer and prove ROI)
- **Your manager** (another Applied AI Engineer or someone leading the field team)
- **Internal product/engineering team** (occasionally: when a customer hits a Devin limitation or wants a feature, you advocate for them)

**Weekly/monthly collaboration:**
- **Workshops and enablement sessions** (you're teaching, not lecturing; hands-on, real problems)
- **Cross-customer learning** (monthly team sync where Applied AI Engineers compare playbooks: "Here's what worked at fintech company X vs. SaaS company Y")

---

## **The Reality Check: What Makes This Role Different**

This is **not** a customer success or account management role. It's technical execution + strategic thinking:
- **Technical**: You're debugging Devin's behavior in production codebases, writing MCP configs, understanding why Devin hallucinated in one specific codebase context.
- **Strategic**: You're thinking about leverage—how do you turn this customer's win into a playbook 100 other teams can use? How do you measure and communicate ROI in ways that make expansion inevitable?

The hardest part: **Devin still isn't fully autonomous.** The honest reality from the internet chatter is that Devin sometimes goes off-track, needs course correction, and works best with guardrails. Your job is making those guardrails *invisible to the customer*—architecting workflows, configurations, and training so it *feels* like natural collaboration, not babysitting.

---

## **Why This Role Exists Now**

Cognition just closed $400M. They have Devin working at some of the world's biggest institutions, but adoption is still being figured out case-by-case. A year from now, they want to hire 10 more Applied AI Engineers. But first, you're turning ad-hoc customer success stories into playbooks. You're not just onboarding customers; you're building the **operating manual for AI adoption at scale**.

The pay ($180–225K + equity) reflects that you're one of the first, the learning curve is steep, and you're directly tied to Cognition's ability to expand.

Does this match the reality you were expecting, or do you have questions about specific parts of the role?
